chunks.text: The Principal-Agent Alignment Problem in Artificial Intelligence | EECS at UC Berkeley The Principal-Agent Alignment Problem in Artificial Intelligence Dylan Hadfield-Menell EECS Department, University of California, Berkeley Technical Report No. UCB/EECS-2021-207 August 26, 2021 http://www2.eecs.berkeley.edu/Pubs/TechRpts/2021/EECS-2021-207.pdf The field of artificial intelligence has seen serious progress in recent years, and has also caused seriousĂ‚ concernsĂ‚ thatĂ‚ rangeĂ‚ fromĂ‚ theĂ‚ immediateĂ‚ harmsĂ‚ causedĂ‚ byĂ‚ systemsĂ‚ thatĂ‚ replicate harmful biases to the more distant worry that effective goal-directed systems may, at a certain level of performance,Ă‚ be able to subvert meaningful control efforts.Ă‚ In this dissertation,Ă‚ I argue the following thesis: 1.Ă‚ TheĂ‚ useĂ‚ ofĂ‚ incompleteĂ‚ orĂ‚ incorrectĂ‚ incentivesĂ‚ toĂ‚ specifyĂ‚ theĂ‚ targetĂ‚ behaviorĂ‚ forĂ‚ an autonomousĂ‚ systemĂ‚ createsĂ‚ a valueĂ‚ alignmentĂ‚ problem betweenĂ‚ theĂ‚ principal(s),Ă‚ on whose behalf a system acts, and the system itself; 2.Ă‚ This value alignment problem can be approached in theory and practice through the development of systems that are responsive to uncertainty about the principalĂ˘s true, unobserved, intended goal; and 3.Ă‚ Value alignment problems can be modeled as a class of cooperative assistance games, which are computationally similar to the class of partially-observable Markov decision processes.Ă‚ This model captures the principalĂ˘s capacity to behave strategically in coordination with the autonomous system.Ă‚ It leads to distinct solutions to alignment problems, comparedĂ‚ withĂ‚ moreĂ‚ traditionalĂ‚ approachesĂ‚ toĂ‚ preferenceĂ‚ learningĂ‚ likeĂ‚ inverse reinforcement learning, and demonstrates the need for strategically robust alignment solutions.
